---
title: Template Everything
---

*Calepin* is built on one idea: **templates everywhere**. Every piece of output --- from a single callout box to a full website --- is controlled by a template you can read, copy, and customize. No Rust, no Lua, no Python, no shell scripts. Just text files with `{% raw %}{{variable}}{% endraw %}` placeholders.

Learn one syntax and you can change how *Calepin* renders anything.


# Why templates?

Most document systems require you to write code in a specific language when you want to customize output. Quarto uses Lua filters. R Markdown uses Pandoc templates and custom output formats written in R. LaTeX requires learning a macro language. Each customization point has its own API, its own learning curve, and its own failure modes.

*Calepin* takes a different approach. Templates are plain text files with a simple substitution syntax ([Jinja](jinja.qmd)). You write the exact output you want --- HTML, LaTeX, Typst, Markdown --- and mark where dynamic content should go with `{% raw %}{{variable}}{% endraw %}`. That's it.

Want to change how callout boxes look? Drop a file in `_calepin/elements/`. Want to wrap your document in a different HTML layout? Drop a file in `_calepin/templates/`. Want to insert a page break or a video? Call a built-in function. The syntax is the same everywhere.


# Four layers of templates

*Calepin* uses templates at four levels, from smallest to largest scope. Each level uses the same Jinja syntax, so what you learn for one applies to all.

## 1. Shortcuts

Shortcuts are Jinja expressions you write directly in your `.qmd` body. They insert content, call functions, or use control flow --- without touching any external files.

For example, to insert a page break that works in every output format:

```
{% raw %}{{ pagebreak() }}{% endraw %}
```

Or to show a paragraph only in HTML:

````
{% raw %}{% if format == "html" %}
This paragraph only appears in HTML output.
{% endif %}{% endraw %}
````

Or to insert the document title:

```
{% raw %}The title is: {{ meta.title }}{% endraw %}
```

Shortcuts give you variables, functions, conditionals, and loops right in your prose. See [Shortcuts](shortcuts.qmd) for the full reference.


## 2. Element templates

Every rendered element --- code blocks, figures, fenced divs, bracketed spans, theorems, callouts --- passes through an element template. These are small files that control the markup for a single component.

For example, the built-in template for `div.html`:

```html
{% raw %}<div class="{{classes}}"{{id_attr}}>
{{children}}
</div>{% endraw %}
```

To customize how a fenced div with class `.highlight-box` renders, create `_calepin/elements/highlight-box.html`:

```html
{% raw %}<div class="highlight-box" style="background: {{color}}; padding: 1em;"{{id_attr}}>
<strong>{{title}}</strong>
{{children}}
</div>{% endraw %}
```

Now any `::: {.highlight-box title="Note" color="#e8f4f8"} ... :::` uses your template. See [Elements](elements.qmd) for all available variables and naming conventions.


## 3. Page templates

The page template wraps the entire rendered body in a document shell --- the `<html>`, `<head>`, CSS, and metadata blocks for HTML; the `\documentclass`, preamble, and `\begin{document}` for LaTeX; the equivalent for Typst.

To use a custom HTML layout, create `_calepin/templates/calepin.html`:

```html
{% raw %}<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8">
<title>{{plain_title}}</title>
{{css}}
</head>
<body>
<h1>{{title}}</h1>
{{body}}
</body>
</html>{% endraw %}
```

This is how you create blog layouts, slide shells, dashboard wrappers, or branded reports without writing code. See [Pages](pages.qmd) for all page template variables.


## 4. Site templates

When you use `calepin site`, *Calepin* renders a collection of `.qmd` files into a structured website with navigation, search, and a consistent layout. The site system has its own set of templates: `base.html`, `page.html`, `listing.html`, `navbar.html`, and more.

To override any site template, create files in `_templates/` at your project root. See [Sites](sites.qmd) for configuration and customization.


## 5. YAML preamble

The YAML front matter at the top of every `.qmd` file controls metadata (title, author, date), output options (format, table of contents, bibliography), and custom variables. For sites, the `_calepin.yaml` file provides project-wide defaults.

```yaml
---
title: My Document
author: Jane Doe
format: html
toc: true
variables:
  version: 2.0
---
```

See [YAML](yaml.qmd) for all supported fields and composition patterns.


# Resolution order

All templates follow a consistent lookup order:

1. **Plugin-provided** (in plugin order)
2. **Project** (`_calepin/elements/` or `_calepin/templates/`)
3. **User** (`~/.config/calepin/elements/` or `~/.config/calepin/templates/`)
4. **Built-in** (compiled into the binary)

The first match wins. This means you can override any built-in template by placing a file with the right name in your project's `_calepin/` directory --- no need to fork or rebuild anything.
